=== TECHNICAL SEO SETTINGS ===

Meta title: ERISA Compliance Support | PointOak Retirement Advisors

Meta description: PointOak delivers ERISA compliance support with named fiduciary oversight, fee benchmarking, and governance documentation for plan sponsors. Schedule a consultation.

URL: https://www.pointoak.com/erisa-compliance-support/

Schema markup needed: Service, FAQPage, BreadcrumbList, HowTo

ERISA Compliance Support for Retirement Plan Sponsors

Plan sponsors retain fiduciary responsibilities no recordkeeper covers. The DOL's Employee Benefits Security Administration oversees roughly 801,000 private industry retirement plans covering about 156 million participants and families. PointOak Retirement Advisors delivers ERISA compliance support with named fiduciary oversight and governance documentation through our retirement plan consulting services.

  • Named 3(21) and 3(38) ERISA fiduciary

  • 450+ data points reviewed per plan bidder

  • Fee-only fiduciary since 2017

Benefits of ERISA Compliance Support

Reduced Fiduciary Liability Exposure

EBSA restored nearly $1.4 billion to retirement plans in FY 2025 and referred 75 cases for litigation (EBSA monetary results). Documented fiduciary support proves your committee acted in plan participants' best interests. That record positions plan fiduciaries favorably when the DOL or litigants raise questions.

Earlier Error Detection Before Enforcement Escalates

IRS guidance allows self-correction of operational failures, but only when established compliance procedures are in place. A plan document does not satisfy that standard. Ongoing monitoring catches issues while voluntary correction paths remain, helping you avoid costly penalties and minimize risk before compliance deadlines pass.

A Documented Governance Process for Your Committee

The DOL requires fiduciaries to prudently select and monitor service providers. Hiring a recordkeeper does not eliminate that duty. Structured committee support, meeting minutes, and documented plan administrator decisions create the audit trail satisfying ERISA's prudence standard and ensure compliance with ongoing oversight.

Defined Responsibility Boundaries Between Sponsor and Providers

Most employee benefit plans involve multiple service providers, and fiduciary duties do not transfer automatically between them. PointOak maps what stays with the sponsor, what gets delegated, and what falls through the gap. Clear responsibility boundaries prevent assumptions that leave critical oversight tasks unassigned.

Independent Fee Analysis That Protects Plan Participants

Seventy-four percent of DC plan sponsors said reviewing plan fees was their top focus in 2024. Independent provider and fee benchmarking uses open-market data rather than vendor self-reporting to satisfy ERISA's fee-reasonableness standard. That analysis protects plan assets and fiduciaries responsible for them.

How ERISA Compliance Support Works

ERISA compliance support operates as a continuous monitoring and governance system, not periodic filing assistance. Three operational layers drive it: fiduciary monitoring, document and procedure governance, and correction with ongoing support. Under IRS rules, self-correction requires established compliance procedures beyond a written plan document and summary plan description.

The DOL requires participant-level disclosure before first investment direction, annually, and with actual fee data at least quarterly for participant-directed plans (DOL fiduciary guidance). That cadence makes compliance support an ongoing operational function. Each cycle involves investment analysis, fee review, and governance documentation tied to current ERISA regulations and Department of Labor requirements.

Schedule a consultation to review your plan's ERISA compliance and fiduciary governance.

Who Needs ERISA Compliance Support

Not every plan sponsor needs the same level of outside support. These scenarios signal when independent compliance oversight adds value.

Plan Sponsors Relying on Recordkeepers for Fiduciary Monitoring

Your recordkeeper handles transactions, not fiduciary monitoring. Private employers and private sector employers in this position carry unmanaged risk when assuming bundled vendors cover all ERISA compliance.

HR Leaders Preparing for a DOL Review or IRS Audit

Internal documentation is incomplete or procedures are not formalized. A DOL review requires evidence of a functioning governance process across the full plan year with organized records and documented decisions.

Fiduciary Committees Without Formal Governance Documentation

No committee charter, documented selection process, or structured minutes means IRS self-correction eligibility is at risk. Education and communications programs for committee members build the procedures required for voluntary correction.

Organizations That Have Not Benchmarked Plan Fees Recently

ERISA's fee-reasonableness standard requires periodic benchmarking. If your last independent review was two or more years ago, exposure exists. Employers owe employees a retirement plan priced at reasonable levels, and regulators expect the data to prove it.

Sponsors Managing Plans Under Expanding ERISA Requirements

DOL's cybersecurity guidance and ongoing SECURE 2.0 implementation widen what compliance covers for clients across industries. Internal teams may lack bandwidth to track regulatory changes, updated plan design requirements, and new disclosure obligations simultaneously.

When an alternative is better: If you operate only a SEP-IRA or SIMPLE IRA without ERISA obligations or need one-time legal representation for a specific dispute, a specialized ERISA attorney may be a better fit.

ERISA Compliance Support vs. Bundled Vendor Administration

Both models can serve plan sponsors, but the right choice depends on plan complexity, who controls plan assets, and how fiduciary monitoring responsibilities are handled.

Feature

Bundled Vendor Administration

Independent Compliance Advisory

Independence from plan providers

Tied to parent vendor's products and economics

No vendor affiliation; benchmarks across all providers

Fee benchmarking

Self-reported or limited to own marketplace

Open-market analysis using actual plan data

Ongoing governance cadence

Typically annual review around Form 5500

Quarterly or semi-annual governance with committee support

Responsibility mapping

Often unclear what remains the sponsor's duty

Documented delegation vs. retained duties for plan fiduciaries

Correction and remediation

May flag issues but limited correction guidance

Coordinates VFCP, SCC, and IRS self-correction pathways

Bundled administration can work when plans are straightforward and your internal team has governance experience. When complexity grows, committees rotate, or benchmarking has not been independently verified, advisory provider and fee benchmarking and fiduciary oversight help sponsors comply with ERISA's monitoring standard.

What To Expect: Our ERISA Compliance Support Process

Step 1: Review Your Plan Documents and Compliance Procedures

We review your plan documents, governance records, and compliance procedures against ERISA requirements and IRS qualification rules.

Step 2: Identify Governance Gaps and Fiduciary Exposure

You receive a clear report mapping covered areas, retained plan administrator duties, and gaps creating exposure.

Step 3: Develop Targeted Remediation and Governance Recommendations

We recommend corrections and benchmarking actions prioritized by risk, including voluntary compliance programs where applicable.

Step 4: Implement Changes With Ongoing Monitoring

Your committee receives fiduciary support training, documentation, and recurring reviews to prevent missed deadlines.

Why Choose PointOak for ERISA Compliance Support

The DOL received 797 Voluntary Fiduciary Correction Program applications in FY 2025 (EBSA enforcement and correction data). That volume confirms plan errors are common and enforcement is active. PointOak helps sponsors identify issues before formal correction is required and coordinates the pathway through VFCP, SCC, or IRS self-correction when it is.

Plan sponsors often assume their recordkeeper handles compliance, but TPAs do not absorb fiduciary monitoring duties under labor law. PointOak serves as a named fiduciary under ERISA, accepting defined responsibilities documented in writing. See how that structure works through our consulting team and about PointOak.

About PointOak Retirement Advisors

PointOak Retirement Advisors is an SEC-registered, fee-only investment advisory firm founded in 2017 and headquartered in McLean, Virginia. We provide corporate retirement plan consulting and wealth management nationwide with over $1.08 billion in assets under management. Our team brings 50+ years of collective experience, and Darsh Makim's practice dates to 2005. Named 3(21) and 3(38) ERISA fiduciary. Schedule a consultation.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does ERISA compliance support cost?

Fees depend on plan complexity, asset size, and scope of fiduciary services.

PointOak's fee-only model means no commissions or hidden revenue-sharing. Pricing varies based on the number of plans, total assets, governance scope, and whether the engagement includes named fiduciary services under the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974. Most plan sponsors find that independent compliance support costs less than a single DOL correction or fee-litigation settlement. PointOak provides a transparent fee proposal after the initial plan review, covering all benefit plans in scope.

What is the difference between a compliance advisor and a bundled vendor's compliance support?

A compliance advisor provides independent oversight; a bundled vendor administers the plan.

Bundled vendors handle recordkeeping but typically do not accept fiduciary responsibility for governance. An independent advisor benchmarks fees, documents committee decisions, and accepts named fiduciary duties. ERISA is a federal law that sets minimum standards for pension plans and other employee benefit plans, and independent advisors help sponsors meet those standards.

Is ERISA compliance support a one-time project or an ongoing process?

ERISA compliance is ongoing; plan sponsors have recurring fiduciary duties every year.

The DOL requires ongoing provider monitoring, periodic fee disclosure, and annual reporting. PointOak's engagement includes recurring governance reviews tied to your plan year cycle, not just initial remediation. Each review updates your plan description SPD compliance, investment menu analysis, and documentation so your fiduciary file stays current.

Who is responsible for ERISA compliance in a 401(k) plan?

The plan sponsor and named fiduciaries bear primary ERISA compliance responsibility.

Even when tasks are delegated to a recordkeeper or TPA, the sponsor retains the duty to monitor those providers and verify fee reasonableness. Hiring outside help does not eliminate oversight obligations. The Employee Benefits Security Administration within the Department of Labor enforces these requirements and can review any plan sponsor's governance practices.

Can ERISA compliance be outsourced?

Parts of ERISA compliance can be outsourced, but core fiduciary duties remain with the sponsor.

A named 3(21) or 3(38) fiduciary can accept defined responsibilities under the act. The sponsor still must prudently select and monitor those providers. Most plan sponsors already use outsourced fiduciary oversight for coverage of key governance functions. PointOak documents what transfers and what stays, creating clear accountability under the Internal Revenue Code.

What does ERISA compliance support typically include?

It covers fiduciary governance, fee benchmarking, document review, corrections, and committee support.

Typical services include Form 5500 coordination, investment menu monitoring, fee benchmarking, plan document and summary plan description review, correction support, and committee facilitation. Scope varies by plan size and complexity. Any material modification triggers updated disclosure, and wrap documents consolidate multiple welfare benefit plans into a single plan description for ERISA reporting.

Do recordkeepers or TPAs handle all ERISA compliance?

Recordkeepers handle administration, but fiduciary monitoring and oversight remain with the sponsor.

A recordkeeper manages transactions, statements, and reporting. That is not the same as overseeing the plan's investment menu, benchmarking fees, documenting governance decisions, or ensuring correction procedures are in place. Those fiduciary duties require separate oversight, and sponsors who assume their vendor handles everything expose themselves and their beneficiaries to unmanaged risk.

Why is ERISA compliance support more than just Form 5500 filing?

Form 5500 is one annual requirement inside a year-round fiduciary obligation.

Plan sponsors owe ongoing provider monitoring, periodic fee disclosure to participants, investment menu oversight, and documented committee governance. The Employee Retirement Income Security Act sets standards for plan administration that extend across every quarter, not just filing season. Treating compliance as a filing exercise leaves the rest of the obligation unmanaged.

How should a plan sponsor evaluate an ERISA compliance support provider?

Evaluate responsibility mapping, monitoring cadence, correction capability, and independence.

Ask whether the provider will accept named fiduciary status, how often governance reviews occur, whether fee benchmarking uses open-market data, and how errors are corrected. Some sponsors seek legal counsel for specific dispute resolution, a separate function from ongoing compliance. PointOak provides a documented responsibility matrix and quarterly or semi-annual governance reviews.

Can retirement-plan errors be self-corrected?

Many operational errors can be self-corrected, but plan document failures generally cannot.

The IRS allows self-correction for eligible operational failures when the plan sponsor has established compliance procedures in place. A plan document alone does not qualify. Document failures typically require formal correction through VFCP or VCP. EBSA's Self Correction Component received 176 applications in FY 2025, confirming sponsors actively use these pathways.

Schedule your consultation to discuss your plan's ERISA compliance and fiduciary governance.
